transcript:
Right now you're watching a warm orange nebula get slowly stripped away, leaving nothing but pale blue-white threads tracing loops and figure-eights in the dark — like watching a fire burn down to just the smoke patterns it left behind.

Here's the thing that got me: those glowing orange orbs aren't decoration, they're literally named "intentions" in the code — each one has a property called presence that starts at 1 and decays to zero on its own timer, in a function called "myWithdrawal." Meanwhile every particle has a "learnedVx" and "learnedVy" — velocity it copied while the intention was still pulling on it. Once presence hits zero, there's a line that blends in "learnedVy times inheritanceFactor" — the particle just keeps orbiting a center that's no longer there. It's not improvising. It's remembering.

So what you're actually seeing in that final all-white frame isn't chaos filling a vacuum — it's momentum, still circling empty space where guidance used to be.
